Unit 2 Project

penguins <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(flipper_length_mm,
          body_mass_g,
          bill_length_mm,
          bill_depth_mm,
          sex)

#Lecture 01 Q1 By adding them_minimal() will remove the grey background and the grid box. Biscoe has the most penguins.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Penguins by Island",
    x = "Island",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Q2 The distribution is bimodal and looks smoother and generalized with fewer bins and more bins showing more variation.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Penguin Body Mass",
    x = "Body Mass (g)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Penguin Body Mass",
    x = "Body Mass (g)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Penguin Body Mass",
    x = "Body Mass (g)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The distribution does differ between species as Gentoo penguins have a higher body mass while Adelie penguins have lower body mass. The species also do not share the same approximate mean.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass Distribution by Species",
    x = "Body Mass (g)",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Q3 The species with the longest flippers are the Gentoo penguins.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm)) + 
geom_boxplot() + 
labs(
title = "Flipper length by species",
x = "species", 
y = "Flipper length (mm)"
) +
theme_minimal()

This boxplot will compare the body mass between males and females. Males showed a higher body mass than females, but there is a bit of overlap.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass by Sex",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Using the fill = species will improve the readability by making each species easier to distinguish.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Flipper Length by Species",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Flipper Length (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Q4 The relationship is shown to be negative which means the penguins will longer bils have shallower bill depths.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Bill Length vs Bill Depth",
    x = "Bill Length (mm)",
    y = "Bill Depth (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

By coloring the points by species we can see the pattern. Each species makes its own cluster, making the negative relationship easier to interpret within species.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Bill Length vs Bill Depth by Species",
    x = "Bill Length (mm)",
    y = "Bill Depth (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Adding a linear regression line helps show the general trend, but there is variability around the line

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Bill Length vs Bill Depth with Linear Fit",
    x = "Bill Length (mm)",
    y = "Bill Depth (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Q5 Gentoo penguins show the strongest positive relationship having body mass increasing as flipper length increases.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Flipper Length vs Body Mass by Species",
    x = "Flipper Length (mm)",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The pattern that appears is males have higher body mass and the positive relationship between flipper length and body mass is more noticeable in Gentoo penguins.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_grid(sex ~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Flipper Length vs Body Mass by Sex and Species",
    x = "Flipper Length (mm)",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Q6 The figure below will show the relationship between flipper length and body mass and will be colored by species and faceted by sex. The plot will show how Gentoo penguins are heavier and have longer flippers compared to the other species, with males being normally larger than females within each species.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ sex) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Flipper Length and Body Mass",
    x = "Flipper Length (mm)",
    y = "Body Mass (g)",
    color = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Q7 Something interesting I liked about this exercise was seeing faceting and color coding can change how patterns are illustrated in the data. The most difficult part was just getting used to writing the code I use after each question as previous assignments just had me write down the code.

#Lecture 02

penguins_messy <- read.csv("penguins_messy.csv")

Q1

str_detect(penguins_messy$species_messy, "adelie") 
##   [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [13]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [25]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [37]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [49]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [61]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [73]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [85]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [97]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [109]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [121]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [133]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [145]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [157] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [169] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [181] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [193] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [205] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [217] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [229] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [241] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [253] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [265] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [277] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [289] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [301] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [313] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [325] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [337] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE

This command checks every value in the species_messy column and returns back TRUE if the text has “adalie” and returns FALSE if not.

str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?", "")
##   [1] "M" "f" "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  NA  NA  NA  "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
##  [19] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [37] "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [55] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [73] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f"
##  [91] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [109] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [127] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [145] "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [163] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  "M"
## [181] "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f"
## [199] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [217] "f" "M" NA  "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [235] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
## [253] "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  "M"
## [271] "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [289] "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [307] "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [343] "M" "f"

This command removes all question marks from the sex_messy column and replace it with an empty string.

str_squish(penguins_messy$island_messy)
##   [1] "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen"
##   [7] "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen"

## [301] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"    
## [307] "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"     "Dream"

## [343] "Dream"     "Dream"

This command will remove extra spaces at the beginning and end of the text and make multiple spaces or tabs into one space in the island_messy column. The number that shows is 316. Q2

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(valid_id = str_detect(sample_id, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))

penguins_messy %>%
  pull(valid_id) %>%
  sum()
## [1] 316

Q3

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  filter(valid_id)

Q4

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(
    file_date = str_extract(file_name, "^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}"),
    file_id = str_extract(file_name, "PAL-[0-9]{3}")
  )

head(penguins_messy$file_date, 10)
##  [1] "2024-01-14" "2024-01-17" "2024-01-08" "2024-01-26" "2024-01-06"
##  [6] "2024-01-08" "2024-01-08" "2024-01-19" "2024-01-14" "2024-01-24"
head(penguins_messy$file_id, 10)
##  [1] "PAL-535" "PAL-442" "PAL-668" "PAL-920" "PAL-223" "PAL-899" "PAL-268"
##  [8] "PAL-139" "PAL-847" "PAL-238"

Q5

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(island = str_squish(island_messy))

unique(penguins_messy$island)
## [1] "Torgersen" "BIS-COE"   "Dream"

Q6

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(
    sex = sex_messy %>%
      str_replace_all("\\?", "") %>%
      str_to_upper()
  )

unique(penguins_messy$sex)
## [1] "M" "F" NA

Q7

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(
    bill_mm = notes %>%
      str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>%
      as.numeric()
  )
## Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
## ℹ In argument: `bill_mm = notes %>% str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>%
##   as.numeric()`.
## Caused by warning in `notes %>% str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>% as.numeric()`:
## ! NAs introduced by coercion

Q8 Something from this exercise that I found interesting to learn was how there are certain expressions that can easily clean up messy biological data. Something challenging in this exercise was memorizing all the regex patterns and what each one does.

#Lecture 03

Q1 The original penguins data set had 344 rows, but with this code removing rows that have missing values in bill_length_mm and sex, there are now only 333 rows.

penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm, sex)

nrow(penguins)
## [1] 333
nrow(penguins_clean)
## [1] 333

Q2 By including duplicates can mess up summary statistics and plots because it can bias means, standard deviations, and cause some measurements to seem more common.

penguins_dup <- penguins_clean %>%
  bind_rows(slice_sample(penguins_clean, n = 30, replace = TRUE))

nrow(penguins_dup)
## [1] 363
penguins_dup %>%
  distinct() %>%
  nrow()
## [1] 333

Q3 There are 8 columns in the data frame.

penguins_selected <- penguins_clean %>%
  select(species, island, sex, where(is.numeric))

ncol(penguins_selected)
## [1] 8

Q4

# Select only bill measurement columns
penguins_clean %>%
  select(starts_with("bill"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1           39.1          18.7
##  2           39.5          17.4
##  3           40.3          18  
##  4           36.7          19.3
##  5           39.3          20.6
##  6           38.9          17.8
##  7           39.2          19.6
##  8           41.1          17.6
##  9           38.6          21.2
## 10           34.6          21.1
## # ℹ 323 more rows
# Select only columns that end with "_mm"
penguins_clean %>%
  select(ends_with("_mm"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
# Select bill and flipper columns using regex
penguins_clean %>%
  select(matches("bill|flipper"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows

Q5 Using across(where(is.numeric), …) is better than typing each numeric column by hand because it reduces errors, keeps the code neater, and adapts automatically if columns are added or deleted.

penguins_clean %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), mean))
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   species   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Adelie              38.8          18.3              190.       3706. 2008.
## 2 Chinstrap           48.8          18.4              196.       3733. 2008.
## 3 Gentoo              47.6          15.0              217.       5092. 2008.

Q6

penguins_clean <- penguins_clean %>%
  mutate(
    bill_length = case_when(
      bill_length_mm < 40 ~ "short",
      bill_length_mm < 50 ~ "medium",
      bill_length_mm >= 50 ~ "long"
    )
  )

Q7 The number of columns in penguins_instrumented is 12 and there are 9 columns in penguins_clean.

instrument_meta <- tibble(
  instrument_id = c("caliper_A", "caliper_B", "caliper_C"),
  calibration_mm = c(0.2, 0.5, 1.0),
  manufacturer = c("Mitutoyo", "Fowler", "Generic")
)
penguins_instrumented <- penguins_clean %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm) %>%
  mutate(
    instrument_id = case_when(
      year == 2007 ~ "caliper_A",
      year == 2008 ~ "caliper_B",
      year == 2009 ~ "caliper_C"
    )
  ) %>%
  left_join(instrument_meta, by = "instrument_id")

ncol(penguins_clean)
## [1] 9
ncol(penguins_instrumented)
## [1] 12

Q8 The most interesting part of this exercise was learning about the across(where(is.numeric), …) function as it makes it a lot better to organize and control your data. Something that was difficult about this exercise
